sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: - Level 1, step 1 - fresh start - Board shows a gray background with several colored objects - Need to identify all objects and their relationships - Valid actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand all objects on the board, then determine the goal and appropriate actions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good, the LEFT action moved the player. Let me check the new positions. The green block should have moved left by 4 columns. I need to map out where all the objects are and figure out how to reach the three blue boxes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['id'] not in [0, 11]:  # skip background and HUD
        print(f"  id={n['id']:2d} color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Map out the layout:
# Blue boxes: 
#   - Left: node 3 (c frame) at ~[28,16]-[31,19], blue center node 5 at [29,17]-[30,18]
#   - Center: node 4 (b frame) at ~[28,28]-[31,39], gray bar node 6 at [29,29]-[30,38]
#   - Right: node 1 (c frame) at ~[24,44]-[27,47], blue center node 2 at [25,45]-[26,46]
#   - Below center: node 7 (c frame) at ~[36,32]-[39,35], blue center node 8 at [37,33]-[38,34]
# Player (green): should now be at cols ~28-31 after LEFT
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving LEFT:
- The green block (node 10) is now at boundary [[40, 29], [40, 31], [43, 31], [43, 29]] - so it's at rows 40-43, cols 29-31
- The white block (node 9) is at [[40, 28], [43, 28]] - a vertical strip at col 28, rows 40-43
- A new node 12 appeared: color=c at [[63, 63]] - this seems like part of the HUD
The player moved from cols 32-35 to cols 29-31 (moved left by about 3 columns). The white block is now to the left of the green block.
Now I see the layout more clearly:
- Left blue box: cols 16-19, rows 28-31
- Center horizontal bar: cols 28-39, rows 28-31
- Right blue box: cols 44-47, rows 24-27
- Below-center blue box: cols 32-35, rows 36-39
- Player: cols 29-31, rows 40-43
The player is now near the left side. Let me try moving UP to see if I can reach the left blue box or the center horizontal bar.
Actually, wait - looking at the layout again:
- The center horizontal bar (node 4) is at cols 28-39, rows 28-31. It's blue (b) with a gray (g) interior.
- The left blue box (node 3) is at cols 16-19, rows 28-31.
- The player is at cols 29-31, rows 40-43.
If I move UP, the player would go to rows 36-39, which is where the below-center blue box is (node 7). But we already know that's blocked from above.
Wait, actually the player is now at cols 29-31, not 32-35. So the player is no longer directly below the below-center blue box (which is at cols 32-35). Let me try moving UP now.
Actually, I need to think about this more carefully. The player moved LEFT and is now at a different column. The below-center blue box is at cols 32-35. The player is at cols 29-31. So if I move UP, the player would go to rows 36-39, cols 29-31, which doesn't overlap with the blue box at cols 32-35.
But there's also the center horizontal bar at rows 28-31, cols 28-39. If I move UP twice, the player would be at rows 32-35, cols 29-31 after the first UP, and then rows 28-31, cols 29-31 after the second UP - which would overlap with the center horizontal bar!
Let me try moving UP once to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is now at cols 29-31, rows 40-43 (shifted left)
# Try UP to see if we can pass to the left of the below-center blue box
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
